officialText: No compensation awarded, penalty imposed, or confiscation made under this Act shall prevent the imposition of any other punishment to which the person affected is liable under any other law for the time being in force.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that penalties, compensation, or confiscation under the IT Act do not prevent additional criminal or civil action under other laws.
examples: A hacker may be liable under both the IT Act and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
Confiscation of equipment does not prevent criminal prosecution.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished under another law as well?
faqs.answer: Yes. Action under the IT Act does not prevent proceedings under other applicable laws.

title: Compounding of Offences
officialText: A court of competent jurisdiction may compound offences under this Act, other than offences punishable with imprisonment for a term exceeding three years or offences affecting the socio-economic conditions of the country or involving a child below the age of eighteen years.
simpleExplanation: This section permits settlement of certain offences under the IT Act, subject to legal limitations.
examples: A minor cyber offence may be settled through compounding.
Serious offences such as cyber terrorism cannot be compounded.

faqs.question: Can all IT Act offences be compounded?
faqs.answer: No. Serious offences and offences involving children cannot be compounded.

faqs.question: Who can approve compounding?
faqs.answer: A court of competent jurisdiction.

title: Offences with Three Years Imprisonment to be Bailable
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, offences punishable with imprisonment of three years and above under this Act shall be cognizable and offences punishable with imprisonment of three years or less shall be bailable.
simpleExplanation: This section determines whether certain offences under the IT Act are bailable or cognizable based on the prescribed punishment.
examples: An offence punishable up to three years may generally be bailable.
A more serious offence may be treated as cognizable.

faqs.question: Are all IT Act offences bailable?
faqs.answer: No. Classification depends on the punishment prescribed.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It determines arrest and bail procedures.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to bail and cognizable offences

title: Power to Investigate Offences
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, a police officer not below the rank of Inspector shall investigate any offence under this Act.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies that investigations under the IT Act must be conducted by police officers of at least Inspector rank.
examples: A cybercrime complaint is investigated by a Cyber Crime Inspector.
A Sub-Inspector cannot independently investigate an IT Act offence unless authorized by law.

faqs.question: Who can investigate IT Act offences?
faqs.answer: A police officer not below the rank of Inspector.

faqs.question: Why is a minimum rank prescribed?
faqs.answer: To ensure investigations are handled by sufficiently experienced officers.

title: Exemption from Liability of Intermediary in Certain Cases
officialText: An intermediary shall not be liable for any third-party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by it, provided that the intermediary observes due diligence and complies with the requirements prescribed under this Act and related rules.
simpleExplanation: This section provides 'safe harbor' protection to intermediaries such as social media platforms, hosting providers, and online services, provided they comply with legal requirements.
punishment: Safe harbor protection may be lost if statutory obligations are not met.
examples: A social media platform is generally not liable for user-generated content if it follows due diligence requirements.
An intermediary removes unlawful content after receiving lawful notice.

faqs.question: What is intermediary safe harbor?
faqs.answer: Protection from liability for content posted by users, subject to compliance with legal obligations.

faqs.question: Can safe harbor protection be lost?
faqs.answer: Yes. Failure to comply with due diligence requirements may remove the protection.

title: Power of Police Officer and Other Officers to Enter, Search, etc.
officialText: Any police officer not below the rank of Inspector or any other officer authorized by the Central Government or State Government may enter any public place and search and arrest without warrant any person reasonably suspected of having committed or being about to commit an offence under this Act.
simpleExplanation: This section grants search, seizure, and arrest powers in relation to offences under the IT Act.
examples: A cybercrime suspect is arrested during a lawful search operation.
Investigators seize devices suspected to be involved in cyber offences.

faqs.question: Can arrest be made without a warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes, where the requirements of this section are satisfied.

faqs.question: What places can be searched?
faqs.answer: Public places and locations as permitted under the law.

title: Act to Have Overriding Effect
officialText: The provisions of this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. Provided that nothing contained in this Act shall restrict any person from exercising rights conferred under the Copyright Act, 1957 or the Patents Act, 1970.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the IT Act overriding effect over inconsistent laws while preserving rights available under intellectual property laws.
examples: Where another law conflicts with the IT Act regarding electronic records, the IT Act may prevail.
Copyright and patent rights remain protected despite the overriding clause.

faqs.question: What does overriding effect mean?
faqs.answer: The IT Act prevails over conflicting provisions in other laws.

faqs.question: Are intellectual property rights affected?
faqs.answer: No. Rights under the Copyright Act and Patents Act remain protected.

title: Chairperson, Members, Officers and Employees to be Public Servants
officialText: The Chairperson, Members, officers and employees of the Appellate Tribunal, the Controller, Deputy Controllers, Assistant Controllers and other officers and employees appointed under this Act shall be deemed to be public servants within the meaning of section 21 of the Indian Penal Code.
simpleExplanation: This section treats officials working under the Information Technology Act as public servants, making them subject to legal duties and protections applicable to public officials.
examples: A Controller of Certifying Authorities is treated as a public servant while performing official duties.
Tribunal members are subject to laws applicable to public servants.

faqs.question: Why are these officials considered public servants?
faqs.answer: To ensure accountability and provide legal protection while performing official duties.

faqs.question: Does this status impose responsibilities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Public servants are subject to legal obligations and anti-corruption laws.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to public servants

title: Power to Give Directions
officialText: The Central Government may give directions to any State Government regarding the carrying into execution of the provisions of this Act, and the State Government shall comply with such directions.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Central Government to issue directions to State Governments to ensure effective implementation of the Information Technology Act.
examples: The Central Government issues directions regarding cybersecurity enforcement.
States are instructed to implement specific compliance measures under the Act.

faqs.question: Who can issue directions under this section?
faqs.answer: The Central Government.

faqs.question: Must State Governments comply?
faqs.answer: Yes. State Governments are required to carry out such directions.

title: Protection of Action Taken in Good Faith
officialText: No suit, prosecution or other legal proceeding shall lie against the Central Government, State Government, Controller, Certifying Authority, or any officer for anything done or intended to be done in good faith under this Act or any rules, regulations or orders made thereunder.
simpleExplanation: This section protects officials and authorities from legal action when they act honestly and in good faith while carrying out their duties under the Act.
examples: An official carrying out lawful duties under the Act is protected if acting in good faith.
A Certifying Authority is protected for bona fide actions taken in compliance with the law.

faqs.question: What does good faith mean?
faqs.answer: Acting honestly and without malicious intent while exercising legal authority.

faqs.question: Does this protect unlawful actions?
faqs.answer: No. The protection applies only to actions taken in good faith.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to acts done in good faith

title: Offences by Companies
officialText: Where a company commits an offence under this Act, every person who, at the time the offence was committed, was in charge of and responsible for the conduct of the business of the company shall be deemed guilty and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly. However, such person shall not be liable if he proves that the offence was committed without his knowledge or that he exercised due diligence to prevent it.
simpleExplanation: This section makes company officers responsible for offences committed by the company unless they can show lack of knowledge or that they took reasonable steps to prevent the offence.
punishment: Punishment depends on the underlying offence committed by the company.
examples: A company illegally discloses personal data and responsible executives may face liability.
Directors may be prosecuted if a cybercrime is committed through company operations.

faqs.question: Can company directors be held liable?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they were responsible for the conduct of the company's business.

faqs.question: Is there any defense available?
faqs.answer: Yes. A person may avoid liability by proving lack of knowledge or due diligence.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to corporate liability

title: Removal of Difficulties
officialText: If any difficulty arises in giving effect to the provisions of this Act, the Central Government may, by order published in the Official Gazette, make provisions not inconsistent with this Act for removing the difficulty, provided such power is exercised within the prescribed period.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Central Government to issue orders to address implementation difficulties that may arise under the Act.
examples: The Government issues a clarification to resolve implementation issues after amendments.
Administrative gaps are addressed through official orders.

faqs.question: Why is a removal of difficulties clause included?
faqs.answer: To help resolve unforeseen implementation issues without requiring immediate legislative amendments.

faqs.question: Can the Government override the Act using this power?
faqs.answer: No. Any order must be consistent with the provisions of the Act.

title: Power of Central Government to Make Rules
officialText: The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Act. Such rules may provide for matters required to be prescribed under various provisions of the Act.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the Central Government to frame detailed rules necessary for implementing the Information Technology Act.
examples: The Government issues rules governing electronic signatures.
Cybersecurity compliance requirements are prescribed through rules.

faqs.question: Who can make rules under the IT Act?
faqs.answer: The Central Government.

faqs.question: Why are rules needed?
faqs.answer: Rules provide detailed procedures for implementing the Act.

title: Power of Controller to Make Regulations
officialText: The Controller may, with the previous approval of the Central Government and by notification in the Official Gazette, make regulations consistent with this Act and the rules made thereunder to carry out the purposes of the Act.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes the Controller of Certifying Authorities to make regulations for matters within his jurisdiction.
examples: The Controller issues regulations regarding digital certificate management.
Technical standards are prescribed for Certifying Authorities.

faqs.question: Can the Controller make regulations independently?
faqs.answer: No. Prior approval of the Central Government is required.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of these regulations?
faqs.answer: To facilitate effective implementation of the Act.

title: Laying of Rules and Regulations Before Parliament
officialText: Every rule and regulation made under this Act shall be laid before each House of Parliament while it is in session for a total period of thirty days. Parliament may modify or annul such rule or regulation.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures parliamentary oversight of rules and regulations made under the Information Technology Act.
examples: New IT Rules are presented before Parliament after publication.
Parliament reviews regulations framed under the Act.

faqs.question: Can Parliament modify rules made under the Act?
faqs.answer: Yes. Parliament may modify or annul such rules or regulations.

faqs.question: Why is this requirement included?
faqs.answer: To ensure democratic oversight of delegated legislation.

title: Power of State Government to Make Rules
officialText: The State Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules to carry out provisions of this Act in respect of matters within its jurisdiction, subject to the provisions of the Act and rules made by the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: This section allows State Governments to make rules for matters assigned to them under the Act.
examples: A State Government issues procedural rules for implementation within the state.
State authorities prescribe local compliance mechanisms.

faqs.question: Can State Governments make rules under the IT Act?
faqs.answer: Yes, for matters within their jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Must state rules comply with central rules?
faqs.answer: Yes. State rules cannot conflict with the Act or central rules.

title: Repeal and Savings
officialText: Certain provisions previously governing electronic records and related matters stand repealed, but actions, rights, obligations, liabilities, proceedings, and legal effects accrued under those provisions are preserved.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that repeal of earlier provisions does not invalidate actions already taken under them.
examples: A certificate issued before a legislative change remains valid.
Pending proceedings continue despite repeal of earlier provisions.

faqs.question: What is a savings clause?
faqs.answer: It preserves legal rights and actions despite repeal of earlier provisions.

faqs.question: Do pending proceedings continue?
faqs.answer: Yes. Existing rights and proceedings are generally preserved.

title: Amendment of Certain Enactments
officialText: The enactments specified in the Schedules to this Act shall stand amended in the manner provided therein to recognize electronic records, electronic signatures, and electronic commerce.
simpleExplanation: This section facilitates amendments to other laws so that electronic records and digital transactions receive legal recognition.
examples: Evidence laws are amended to recognize electronic records.
Commercial laws are updated to accommodate electronic transactions.

faqs.question: Why are other laws amended?
faqs.answer: To ensure consistency with electronic governance and digital transactions.

faqs.question: Which laws are affected?
faqs.answer: Various laws specified in the Schedules to the Act.

title: Schedules to the Act
officialText: The First Schedule, Second Schedule, and other Schedules appended to this Act form an integral part of the Act and specify matters such as excluded documents, approved electronic signature techniques, and consequential amendments.
simpleExplanation: This section recognizes the legal importance of the Schedules attached to the Information Technology Act.
examples: The First Schedule lists documents excluded from electronic recognition.
The Second Schedule specifies approved electronic signature methods.

faqs.question: Are the Schedules legally binding?
faqs.answer: Yes. They form part of the Act.

faqs.question: What information is contained in the Schedules?
faqs.answer: Technical specifications, exclusions, and consequential amendments.

title: Continuation of Legal Effect of Electronic Transactions
officialText: Electronic records, electronic signatures, digital certificates, and actions lawfully performed under the provisions of this Act shall continue to enjoy legal recognition and validity in accordance with applicable law.
simpleExplanation: This section reinforces the continuing legal validity of electronic records, signatures, and transactions recognized under the Act.
examples: A valid electronically signed contract remains enforceable.
Digital certificates issued under the Act continue to have legal effect.

faqs.question: Do electronic transactions remain legally valid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Transactions recognized under the Act continue to enjoy legal validity.

faqs.question: Why is this provision important?
faqs.answer: It supports confidence in electronic commerce and governance.
